title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
official_title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework — NIST AI 100-1
status: Published — Voluntary; quasi-mandatory for federal contractors
overview: The NIST AI RMF is a voluntary framework that provides a structured approach to managing risks throughout the AI lifecycle. It is technology- and sector-neutral, applicable to any organisation developing or deploying AI. While voluntary, it has become the de facto US AI governance standard: EO 14110 endorses it, OMB M-24-10 requires federal agencies to implement it, and it is increasingly referenced in sector-specific regulations (FTC, OCC, CFPB). Companies seeking to demonstrate AI governance maturity should align with it.

core_functions.description: Cultivate and implement organisational processes, procedures, and policies for AI risk management. Establish accountability structures, values, and culture for responsible AI.
key_categories: GV-1: Policies, processes, and accountability for AI risk are documented and established
GV-2: Accountability and transparency are established throughout the AI lifecycle
GV-3: Organisational roles and responsibilities for AI risk are defined and in place
GV-4: Organisational teams are committed to a culture of transparency and AI risk management
GV-5: AI risk management processes are informed by and incorporate input from AI actors throughout the lifecycle
GV-6: Policies and procedures are in place to address AI risks and benefits arising from third-party AI technology and data

core_functions.description: Identify and classify AI risks in context. Understand the organisational context, categorise AI systems, and identify affected stakeholders.
key_categories: MP-1: Context is established for the AI risk assessment
MP-2: Scientific understanding of AI risks and benefits is used to establish context
MP-3: AI risks and benefits to individuals, groups, communities, organisations, and society are understood
MP-4: Risks and impacts of the AI system to individuals, groups, communities, and society are assessed

core_functions.description: Analyse, assess, benchmark, and monitor AI risks and their impacts using quantitative and qualitative methods.
key_categories: MS-1: AI risk measurement is established
MS-2: AI systems are evaluated for trustworthy characteristics
MS-3: AI risks are tracked in the context of real-world deployment
MS-4: Feedback about efficacy of risk management practices is gathered and considered

core_functions.description: Allocate resources to treat identified AI risks and respond to incidents and feedback.
key_categories: MG-1: Risks from AI systems are prioritised based on impact and likelihood, and mapped to organisational risk tolerance
MG-2: Treatments for identified AI risks are prioritised and in place
MG-3: AI risks are monitored over time and addressed
MG-4: Risk treatments including responses to incidents are documented and monitored

playbook_highlights.description: Catalogue all AI systems in use or development with metadata on purpose, data, stakeholders, and risk level
Conduct pre-deployment assessments covering all trustworthy characteristics, particularly fairness and safety
Test AI systems for discriminatory outcomes across demographic groups; document results and mitigations
Define what constitutes an AI incident, who is responsible for response, and how to report to affected parties
Create standardised documentation (model cards) for each AI system covering intended use, limitations, and performance

relationship_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Complementary — ISO 42001 is certifiable and covers similar ground; organisations may pursue ISO 42001 certification while using RMF as implementation guidance
Aligned on risk-based approach; RMF implementation helps satisfy EU AI Act governance requirements for high-risk AI systems
RMF's GOVERN function aligns with CSF governance tier; AI and cybersecurity risk management should be integrated

cross_references.relevance: EO 14110 directs NIST to develop AI safety standards and elevates the RMF as the US government's preferred AI governance framework
Federal agencies required to implement AI governance practices aligned with the RMF; federal contractors increasingly expected to demonstrate RMF alignment
